name: troubleshooting
context: Diagnose DuckDB parser, binder, type inference, CSV, extension, remote file, lock, memory, and performance failures
info: DuckDB failures usually fall into a few buckets: SQL parser or binder errors, file format and schema inference surprises, extension install/load problems, object-storage credentials, memory pressure, locks on `.duckdb` files, and shell quoting mistakes. This troubleshooting node gives agents a triage path with concrete checks and commands before changing code. It links back to [[duckdb/cli]] for execution, [[duckdb/files]] for file-reader options, [[duckdb/extensions]] and [[duckdb/secrets]] for remote data, and [[duckdb/performance]] for OOM and slow queries.
body:
# DuckDB Troubleshooting

Use a narrow triage path. First identify whether the failure is SQL, file inference, extension/credentials, memory/performance, or filesystem locking.

## Fast Triage

```bash
# Check executable and version
duckdb --version

# Reduce to a minimal query
duckdb -c "SELECT 1 AS ok;"

# Inspect file schema before transforming
duckdb -c "DESCRIBE SELECT * FROM read_parquet('data.parquet');"

# Profile if it runs but is slow
duckdb -c "EXPLAIN ANALYZE SELECT count(*) FROM 'data.parquet';"
```

## Parser Errors

Parser errors mean the SQL text is not valid syntax after shell quoting. Common causes: missing semicolon in interactive mode, smart quotes copied from docs, unescaped single quotes in paths, reserved words used as identifiers, and shell expansion of `*` or `$` before DuckDB sees the query.

```bash
# Good: SQL sees the glob string
duckdb -c "SELECT count(*) FROM read_parquet('data/*.parquet');"

# Safer for complex SQL: use a file
duckdb analysis.duckdb < report.sql
```

## Binder Errors

Binder errors usually mean a referenced table, column, function, or type cannot be resolved.

```sql
DESCRIBE SELECT * FROM read_csv('input.csv');
SELECT * FROM duckdb_tables();
SELECT * FROM duckdb_columns() WHERE table_name = 'events';
```

If a column exists but has unexpected capitalization or punctuation, quote it: `SELECT "User ID" FROM users;`.

## CSV and Type Inference

CSV auto-detection can guess the wrong delimiter, header, date, or column type. Pin options when the file is important.

```sql
SELECT *
FROM read_csv(
  'input.csv',
  header = true,
  delim = ',',
  sample_size = -1,
  nullstr = ['', 'NULL'],
  union_by_name = true
);
```

For mixed-type columns, cast deliberately after reading as text or provide an explicit schema.

## Extension Failures

```sql
SELECT extension_name, installed, loaded, install_path
FROM duckdb_extensions()
ORDER BY extension_name;

INSTALL httpfs;
LOAD httpfs;
```

If `INSTALL` fails, check network access, repository policy, DuckDB version, platform support, and proxy configuration. If `LOAD` fails, the installed extension may not match the running DuckDB version.

## Remote File and S3 Failures

Separate extension loading from credentials and path issues.

```sql
INSTALL httpfs;
LOAD httpfs;
-- Then create the relevant secret from [[duckdb/secrets]]
SELECT count(*) FROM read_parquet('s3://bucket/path/one-file.parquet');
```

Check endpoint, region, URL style, session token, bucket policy, object prefix, and whether the runtime has outbound network access.

## Out of Memory

Use [[duckdb/performance#memory-and-temp]] first.

```sql
SET memory_limit = '4GB';
SET temp_directory = '/tmp/duckdb-spill';
SET preserve_insertion_order = false;
```

Then reduce selected columns, filter earlier, convert CSV/JSON to Parquet, or persist intermediate tables.

## Database Locks

DuckDB is embedded. Avoid multiple processes writing to the same `.duckdb` file. Use `-readonly` for inspection and serialize writers in scripts or CI jobs.

sections.title: Parser errors
sections.body: Parser errors are syntax or quoting problems. Reproduce with `duckdb -c 'SELECT 1'`, move complex SQL into a `.sql` file, replace smart quotes, check semicolons, quote string literals, and avoid letting the shell expand globs before DuckDB receives them.

sections.title: Binder errors
sections.body: Binder errors mean the SQL parsed but names or types could not be resolved. Run `DESCRIBE SELECT * FROM source`, inspect `duckdb_tables()` and `duckdb_columns()`, qualify ambiguous columns, and quote unusual identifiers. Check whether extension functions are loaded.

sections.title: CSV inference problems
sections.body: Auto-detection can misread headers, delimiters, nulls, dates, and mixed-type columns. Set `header`, `delim`, `sample_size`, `nullstr`, date/timestamp formats, and explicit types for production jobs. Use `sample_size = -1` when a late row changes inference.

sections.title: Extension failures
sections.body: Check `duckdb_extensions()` for installed and loaded state. Re-run `INSTALL name; LOAD name;`. If installation fails, inspect network and repository policy. If loading fails, verify platform and DuckDB version compatibility. Prefer core repositories unless the task explicitly requires community extensions.

sections.title: Remote file failures
sections.body: For HTTPS/S3 problems, first load `httpfs`, then run a minimal one-file query. Verify credentials with [[duckdb/secrets]], then endpoint, region, path style, session token, and bucket permissions. Avoid debugging against a recursive glob until a single object works.

sections.title: Out of memory and slow queries
sections.body: Set `temp_directory`, right-size `memory_limit`, and consider `preserve_insertion_order = false`. Use `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` to identify the expensive operator. Select fewer columns, filter earlier, convert raw files to Parquet, and checkpoint expensive intermediates.

links.context: Reproduce failures with minimal CLI commands
Pin CSV reader options when inference fails
Resolve extension install and load issues
Fix shell quoting around SQL strings

related.note: Performance triage handles slow and out-of-memory workloads
Most ingestion failures are file-reader option issues
Embedded database lock and local file behavior are adjacent operational concerns
